jokes.text: What do we need church shoes for? Jesus wore sandals. / Well, maybe if he had better arch support, they wouldn't have caught him.
jokes.setup: Bart questions need for church shoes by referencing Jesus's footwear
jokes.punchline: Homer suggests Jesus's crucifixion was due to poor athletic performance from inadequate shoes
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of religious reverence with absurd athletic logic. Perfect Homer character moment - taking mundane concern to blasphemous conclusion.

jokes.text: There's that bird you like to argue with. / Well, well, well, if it isn't Professor Know-it-all.
jokes.setup: Marge points out a bird Homer apparently has ongoing disputes with
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately addresses the bird as an intellectual rival
jokes.explanation: Pure Homer absurdity - having intellectual feuds with animals. Good character work showing his petty, delusional nature.

jokes.text: Something for church... but also for doctor's appointments, dental checkups... piano recitals, building dedications... visiting elderly relatives, haircuts and shoe shopping.
jokes.setup: Salesman describes versatile dress shoe for multiple occasions
jokes.punchline: List escalates to absurdly mundane activities like shoe shopping
jokes.explanation: Serviceable escalation joke but fairly predictable. The circular logic of wearing shoes to shop for shoes is mildly clever.

jokes.text: Did you hear that, kids? Brown. / Oh, rats. Talk about bad luck.
jokes.setup: Homer excitedly announces the shoe color to his kids
jokes.punchline: Bart sarcastically treats brown shoes as terrible misfortune
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - Homer's clueless enthusiasm vs Bart's sarcastic disappointment. Economical execution.

jokes.text: I forgot to wear socks today. Guess I can't try those on. / Yeah, no problem. You can wear the store sock.
jokes.setup: Homer claims he can't try on shoes without socks
jokes.punchline: Salesman offers communal store sock, creating hygiene horror
jokes.explanation: Effective gross-out humor. Homer's transparent excuse backfires spectacularly with disgusting solution.

jokes.text: Hey, if you didn't want to tie shoes, you shouldn't have become a shoe salesman.
jokes.setup: Shoe salesman asks customer to lace up shoes due to bad back
jokes.punchline: Bart points out the obvious career mismatch
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart smartass moment. Sharp observational logic delivered with child's bluntness.

jokes.text: It's not enough to want a cracker. You have to earn it.
jokes.setup: Crackling sounds from salesman's back
jokes.punchline: He treats back pain as moral failing requiring effort
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd philosophy about back pain. Character takes physical limitation as character flaw.

jokes.text: Perfect, you'll grow into them. / When? / Oh, you're both way overdue for a spurt.
jokes.setup: Marge justifies oversized shoes as room for growth
jokes.punchline: She claims kids are late for growth spurts like missing appointments
jokes.explanation: Good parental logic satire. Marge treats biological development like scheduled maintenance.

jokes.text: Do you like to laugh? / Oh, yes. As long as it's tasteful. And never at someone, or with, and not- / Excuse me, sir. Do you like to laugh?
jokes.setup: Woman begins elaborate conditions for acceptable humor
jokes.punchline: Promoter immediately abandons her for easier target mid-sentence
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of overly precious comedy gatekeeping. Perfect timing on the interruption.

jokes.text: A rest home for pirates? / No, Dad. It's to fight soil erosion.
jokes.setup: Homer misunderstands the charity cause
jokes.punchline: His version involves elderly seafaring criminals
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd mishearing. Homer's version is infinitely more interesting than soil erosion.

jokes.text: Somebody wanna get this kid a TV? / Whoa, gee, thanks, mister. / Yeah, just joking. I-I'm Jay Leno.
jokes.setup: Leno suggests kid needs TV when he doesn't recognize him
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's joking and is actually the famous host
jokes.explanation: Standard celebrity cameo humor. Works but relies on recognition rather than clever writing.

jokes.text: Didn't he die in a grease fire? / No, he's alive.
jokes.setup: Leno recalls Krusty's supposed demise
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions extremely specific and horrible death
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered casually. The specificity of 'grease fire' makes it funnier than generic death.

jokes.text: Charity, eh? What's my cut? / Nothing? I make more than that takin' a 'schwitz.'
jokes.setup: Krusty asks about payment for charity work
jokes.punchline: Claims he earns more money from sweating than zero dollars
jokes.explanation: Perfect Krusty character moment - mercenary attitude with Yiddish flavor. Absurd comparison works well.

jokes.text: Boy, swipe one pair of Haggar slacks... and you're payin' for it the rest of your life.
jokes.setup: Krusty explains his community service obligation
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's for stealing cheap department store pants
jokes.explanation: Good character development - Krusty's petty criminality. The brand specificity adds authenticity.

jokes.text: I finally got around to reading the dictionary. It turns out the zebra did it.
jokes.setup: Homer announces he read the dictionary
jokes.punchline: Treats it like a mystery novel with zebra as culprit
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer stupidity. Perfect logic failure - treating reference book as narrative with 'Z' entry as resolution. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Dad, the zebra didn't do it. It's just a word at the end of the dictionary. / I still don't get it. / It's just a joke. / Oh, I get it! I get jokes.
jokes.setup: Lisa explains the dictionary isn't a mystery
jokes.punchline: Homer claims understanding after still not understanding
jokes.explanation: Good callback extending the dictionary gag. Homer's fake comprehension is character-perfect.

jokes.text: I got my period today. / Plus, I got this new boyfriend. You know how it is when you're kissing a guy with a tongue stud.
jokes.setup: Female comic begins with TMI announcement
jokes.punchline: Escalates to graphic sexual detail about boyfriend's body modification
jokes.explanation: Deliberately bad comedy to set up Krusty's old-fashioned style. Relies on shock value over craft.

jokes.text: The man Spencer Tracy called 'the most promising newcomer of 1959.'
jokes.setup: Impressive endorsement from legendary actor
jokes.punchline: Quote is from 1959, showing Krusty's career stagnation
jokes.explanation: Clever way to establish Krusty's decline. The date makes the 'promising newcomer' status pathetic.

jokes.text: I tried one the other day. When lightning strikes, the peach cobbler goes out.
jokes.setup: Krusty talks about trying TV dinners
jokes.punchline: Treats dessert like electrical equipment that shorts out
jokes.explanation: Deliberately dated TV dinner material to show Krusty's outdated style. Pun works but feels forced.

jokes.text: You don't have leftovers, you have reruns.
jokes.setup: Continuing TV dinner observations
jokes.punchline: TV/food wordplay connecting leftover food to repeat broadcasts
jokes.explanation: Better than the previous TV dinner joke. Clean pun with good economy, though still dated material.

jokes.text: TV dinner jokes? Ooh! Take that, Swansons.
jokes.setup: Krusty delivers dated TV dinner material
jokes.punchline: Another comedian sarcastically mocks the outdated subject matter
jokes.explanation: Good meta-commentary on Krusty's obsolete material. Brand reference adds specificity.

jokes.text: Me so sorry. Ah, so. / What's he gonna do next, the flapping dickey?
jokes.setup: Krusty does racist Asian stereotype impression
jokes.punchline: Another comedian references equally dated physical comedy prop
jokes.explanation: Shows Krusty's outdated, problematic material. The 'flapping dickey' reference is period-appropriate old comedy.

jokes.text: Me like the fried rice. Me flap dickey long time.
jokes.setup: Krusty continues despite audience disapproval
jokes.punchline: Doubles down with more racist material and prop comedy
jokes.explanation: Deliberately terrible comedy showing Krusty's desperation. Character-appropriate but intentionally unfunny.

jokes.text: Hey, Krusty, great set. Are you kidding? I stunk up the joint. No, no. I was talking to Lisa back there. We both agreed. You killed.
jokes.setup: Bart seems to compliment Krusty's terrible performance
jokes.punchline: Reveals he was talking to Lisa about something else entirely
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing Bart's loyalty while acknowledging the performance was terrible.

jokes.text: Sure, there were a lot of laughs. You probably couldn't hear them. The acoustics were so bad.
jokes.setup: Krusty bombed spectacularly on stage
jokes.punchline: Lisa kindly attributes silence to technical problems
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa kindness - protecting Krusty's feelings with technical excuse. Good character moment.

jokes.text: Did you get a load of 'Corpsey' the Clown? That guy cheapens our whole profession.
jokes.setup: Comedian discusses Krusty's performance
jokes.punchline: Renames him 'Corpsey' to reference his death on stage
jokes.explanation: Sharp nickname playing on 'dying' on stage. Good wordplay with economy.

jokes.text: Last night's comedy benefit delivered wall-to-wall laughs without exception. / The only exception was the embarrassingly dated humor of Springfield's green-haired, red-nosed hack-
jokes.setup: Positive review claims no exceptions to success
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts itself by naming specific exception
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/payoff structure. The contradiction is immediate and devastating to Krusty.

jokes.text: They say any publicity is good publicity. / You, sir, are an idiot.
jokes.setup: Krusty cites optimistic publicity truism
jokes.punchline: His monkey immediately calls him an idiot
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of cliché. Monkey's blunt assessment is economical and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: I don't know what the hell you are. / You can be so cruel when you're sober.
jokes.setup: Krusty expresses confusion about Mel's nature/identity
jokes.punchline: Mel connects Krusty's meanness to his sobriety
jokes.explanation: Good character dynamics. Inverts expectation about alcohol making people mean.

jokes.text: I'm Kent Brockman the clown... filling in for Krusty the Clown who didn't come in today.
jokes.setup: News anchor must substitute for children's entertainer
jokes.punchline: Professional newsman awkwardly tries clown persona
jokes.explanation: Good character fish-out-of-water moment. Kent's professionalism clashes with clown requirements.

jokes.text: He is presumed dead or on vacation.
jokes.setup: Kent explains Krusty's absence
jokes.punchline: Equates death and vacation as equally likely explanations
jokes.explanation: Brilliant deadpan delivery. The casual equivalence of death and vacation is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: It seems a local moron threw his clock out the window. We'll tell you why right after this.
jokes.setup: Kent delivers news-style setup about clock throwing
jokes.punchline: Creates false tension about obvious punchline everyone knows
jokes.explanation: Good parody of news teaser format applied to obvious dad jokes. Meta-humor works well.

jokes.text: Wow, a clown. Do you think he's evil? / He smells evil. / Should we tell Daddy? / No, let's poke him a little while longer.
jokes.setup: Children find unconscious Krusty and debate his morality
jokes.punchline: Decide to continue poking him rather than get help
jokes.explanation: Good child logic - prioritizing entertainment over safety. Dark humor about evil clown trope.

jokes.text: Oh, it hurts to talk. We'll have to work out a system of blinks.
jokes.setup: Krusty's physical pain from drinking
jokes.punchline: Proposes elaborate nonverbal communication system
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy setup. Krusty's dramatic solution to minor problem is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Oh, P.U., what were you drinking, gasoline? / Yes, I was drinking gasoline, Mother.
jokes.setup: Mother asks rhetorical question about bad smell
jokes.punchline: Krusty confirms the extreme literal interpretation
jokes.explanation: Perfect subversion of rhetorical question. Dark humor about Krusty's self-destruction taken to extreme.

jokes.text: Man, look at all the crap with my face on it. / 'Krusty's Personal Swabs'? What was I thinking?
jokes.setup: Krusty surveys his merchandising empire
jokes.punchline: Questions the logic of personal hygiene product endorsement
jokes.explanation: Good satire of inappropriate celebrity endorsements. Cotton swabs are particularly absurd for clown.

jokes.text: Ah, that burns! What the hell is on those things? / Here. Quick, use my Krusty Eye Wash.
jokes.setup: Krusty's own swabs cause him pain
jokes.punchline: Bart offers another Krusty product as solution
jokes.explanation: Good ironic callback - his own products are defective. Bart's innocent suggestion extends the gag.

jokes.text: You mean like when your lazy butler washes your sock garters... and they're still covered with schmutz?
jokes.setup: Leno explains observational comedy about everyday life
jokes.punchline: Krusty's 'universal' example involves butler and sock garters
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Krusty's disconnect from normal life. His 'relatable' problems are hilariously elite.

jokes.text: Dad! / Whoops! Sorry, Son. I didn't know you, Jay Leno and a monkey were bathing a clown.
jokes.setup: Homer accidentally walks in on bathroom scene
jokes.punchline: Casually lists the absurd collection of beings present
jokes.explanation: Brilliant casual treatment of absolutely bizarre situation. Homer's matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Eat your vegetables. Take a sweater. I don't think that's a good idea. Hmm.
jokes.setup: Leno does impression of typical mother sayings
jokes.punchline: Reduces Marge to generic maternal warnings and skepticism
jokes.explanation: Serviceable observational material about maternal behavior. The 'hmm' is a good specific touch.

jokes.text: Have you ever noticed how there are two phone books... a white one and a yellow one? / Like, what's the deal with that? / One's residential, the other is business. / Oh, well, that makes sense.
jokes.setup: Krusty attempts observational comedy about phone books
jokes.punchline: Audience immediately explains the obvious reason, deflating the premise
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-comedy about bad observational comedy. Audience participation destroys the bit perfectly.

jokes.text: What's the deal with cardboard?
jokes.setup: Krusty continues desperate observational attempts
jokes.punchline: Reaches peak desperation with material about basic packaging material
jokes.explanation: Perfect distillation of hack observational comedy. Cardboard is so mundane it becomes absurd subject matter.

jokes.text: Instead of time-tested jokes about women drivers and doctor bills... you got some big-chin schlub... reading typos from the Ralookaville Rost.
jokes.setup: Krusty defends old-fashioned comedy
jokes.punchline: Mocks modern comedy as chin-based typo reading
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of Tonight Show format. 'Big-chin schlub' is specific Leno reference, and fake newspaper name is inspired.

jokes.text: These comics today. 'Ooh, look at me. I can't set my V.C.R. I can't open a bag of airline peanuts. I'm a freakin' moron.'
jokes.setup: Krusty continues ranting about modern comedy
jokes.punchline: Perfectly captures self-deprecating observational comedy style
jokes.explanation: Excellent parody of 90s stand-up comedy tropes. The escalation to 'I'm a freakin' moron' is perfect.

jokes.text: Then you got these lady comics talking about stuff... that would embarrass Redd Foxx- God rest his smutty soul.
jokes.setup: Krusty criticizes female comedians' material
jokes.punchline: References notoriously raunchy comedian as comparison point
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Krusty's old-fashioned sensibilities. Redd Foxx reference is period-appropriate.

jokes.text: What the hell are you laughin' at? I'm just tellin' the truth. / And it's funny. / It is?
jokes.setup: Krusty's rant gets laughs despite not being intended as comedy
jokes.punchline: He discovers his authentic complaints are actually funny
jokes.explanation: Excellent turning point moment. Meta-commentary on how genuine emotion can be funnier than crafted jokes.

jokes.text: Hi, how you folks doing? I'm Moe. Or, as the ladies like to call me, 'Hey, you, behind the bushes.'
jokes.setup: Moe introduces himself to comedy club audience
jokes.punchline: Admits women only know him as threatening lurker
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe character moment - self-aware about his creepiness. Dark humor with great economy.

jokes.text: And now, without further Apu. I have been zinged and I love it.
jokes.setup: Apu tries to say 'without further ado'
jokes.punchline: Makes pun on his own name, then appreciates the wordplay
jokes.explanation: Decent pun with good character moment of Apu appreciating wordplay directed at himself.

jokes.text: The got poor Vincent Price floatin' around on a toilet cake... tellin' me about 'the horrors of an unfresh bowl.'
jokes.setup: Krusty criticizes dead celebrity endorsements
jokes.punchline: Imagines horror movie star selling bathroom products with genre-appropriate language
jokes.explanation: Brilliant combination of celebrity reference with product parody. 'Horrors of an unfresh bowl' is perfect Vincent Price pastiche.

jokes.text: And I tell you somethin' else, I do not believe Winston Churchill... would eat at Der Wienerschnitzel.
jokes.setup: Continues dead celebrity endorsement theme
jokes.punchline: Claims dignified historical figure wouldn't patronize German hot dog chain
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to Vincent Price joke. Churchill/Wienerschnitzel contrast works well.

jokes.text: Well, here's one dollar those crooks aren't gonna get their hands on. / Uh, isn't that illegal? / I don't care if it is illegal. I'm makin' a stand here. Who's with me?
jokes.setup: Krusty burns money to protest corporate greed
jokes.punchline: Declares willingness to break law for symbolic gesture
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Krusty's impulsive nature. Sets up crowd manipulation sequence.

jokes.text: Oh, you're burning it all wrong, Seymour. / It's my allowance, Mother, and I'll burn it the way I want.
jokes.setup: Skinner family joins money burning protest
jokes.punchline: Mother criticizes son's money-burning technique
jokes.explanation: Absolutely perfect character moment. Agnes criticizing Seymour's rebellion technique while he asserts independence is comedy gold.

jokes.text: Here's $42. That's everything I have. / Run home and bury it in the yard. / I love you, Mom.
jokes.setup: Everyone burning money inspired by Krusty
jokes.punchline: Lisa's practical solution to hide money from corporate influence
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's practical approach to anti-corporate sentiment

jokes.text: From now on... I'm gonna be just like Krusty and tell it like it is. / Marge, you're getting a little fat around the old thighs. / Dad! / You too, Bart.
jokes.setup: Homer decides to adopt Krusty's truth-telling approach
jokes.punchline: Immediately insults family members with brutal honesty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - misunderstanding the concept and immediately being tactless. Good escalation pattern.

jokes.text: Oh, knock it off, Homer. You're the fattest one in the car. / You didn't have to tell it like it is, Marge.
jokes.setup: Marge retaliates with equal honesty about Homer's weight
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately regrets his truth-telling philosophy when applied to him
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to Homer's truth-telling. His immediate reversal when targeted is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Well, we think you'd be the perfect spokesman... for our company's new sports utility vehicle, the Canyonero.
jokes.setup: Suits approach anti-corporate Krusty with endorsement deal
jokes.punchline: The vehicle name 'Canyonero' sounds absurdly manufactured
jokes.explanation: Sets up the SUV parody. 'Canyonero' is a good fake product name combining Spanish flair with automotive nonsense.

jokes.text: Oh, well. No! Take your corporate blood wagon and get the hell out. Are you sure? It's free. Stop saying that!
jokes.setup: Krusty wavers when learning the car is free
jokes.punchline: Gets increasingly agitated by repeated mentions of 'free'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Krusty's internal struggle between principles and greed. Sets up his eventual capitulation.

jokes.text: The Canyonero combines the smooth handling of a European sports car... with the rugged drivability of a sturdy 4x4.
jokes.setup: Krusty, who just rejected commercialism, now does car commercial
jokes.punchline: Delivers generic automotive marketing speak about contradictory features
jokes.explanation: Good character reversal showing Krusty's weakness for money. Parodies typical car commercial language.

jokes.text: Fat-free yogurt. The-The quality of computer porn.
jokes.setup: Krusty desperately tries to regain audience with more complaints
jokes.punchline: Topics become increasingly specific and inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Shows Krusty's desperation with increasingly random topics. Computer porn reference is period-appropriate.

jokes.text: It ain't comedy that's in my blood. It's selling out.
jokes.setup: Krusty reflects on his betrayal of principles
jokes.punchline: Admits his true nature is commercialization, not entertainment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character summation. Perfect economy and self-awareness about his true nature as corporate shill.

jokes.text: The Federal Highway Commission has ruled the Canyonero unsafe for highway or city driving.
jokes.setup: Elaborate commercial promoting vehicle
jokes.punchline: Disclaimer reveals it's completely illegal to drive anywhere
jokes.explanation: Perfect puncturing of commercial fantasy. The complete uselessness of the product is revealed in fine print.
